retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Noncompetitive Inhibitor -- Clinical Significance -- Cyanide Poisoning
retrieved_docs.content: Cyanide is a rapidly acting, potentially fatal substance if ingested. Most commonly, cyanide toxicity is the result of smoke inhalation during domestic fires. Toxicity is due to the cessation of oxidative phosphorylation (the production of ATP via oxygen). [6] Cyanide noncompetitively inhibits cytochrome c oxidase, the last enzyme in the electron transport chain. Current treatments for cyanide toxicity focus on intercepting cyanide before it can reach the enzyme or displacing it from the enzyme rather than overcoming the inhibition of the enzyme itself (potentially due to the non-competitive nature of inhibition). [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cyanide toxicity is the result of cessation of aerobic respiration. Cyanide binds to copper and iron moieties in the electron transport chain, inhibiting cytochrome c oxidase activity, a mechanism similar to that of hydrogen sulfide and carbon monoxide. The resulting inhibition of oxidative phosphorylation prevents the production of adenosine triphosphate and redirects energy production to anaerobic respiration pathways, which are rapidly overwhelmed. The pathognomonic lactic acidosis of cyanide toxicity is a direct result of the arrest of aerobic respiration. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Cyanide Antidotes
retrieved_docs.content: Additional cyanide antidotes include sodium nitrite and sodium thiosulfate, which are typically administered in tandem. These are less preferred for multiple reasons. Sodium nitrite produces hypotension as a side effect via vasodilatory-mediated action, which should be avoided in patients already at risk for hemodynamic collapse. Furthermore, sodium nitrite induces methemoglobin, a desired effect in the treatment of cyanide poisoning but with the associated risk of severe morbidity or mortality if high levels of methemoglobin are formed. Sodium thiosulfate has poor penetration to the brain, a location where cyanide is highly active and exerts significant damage on the basal ganglia. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Evaluation and Treatment of Cyanide Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike carbon monoxide, which can be quantified by measuring carboxyhemoglobin level, there is no way to quantify cyanide poisoning. Hyperbaric oxygen (HBO2) is a mainstay of treatment for carbon monoxide poisoning. HBO2 has been recommended for cyanide poisoning as well. It is theorized that HBO2 causes changes in the whole blood cyanide level by hyperoxygenation competing with the cyanide and forcing it out of the cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Evaluation and Treatment of Cyanide Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cyanide, much like carbon monoxide is a cellular asphyxiant. It binds to the enzyme cytochrome c oxidase and blocks the mitochondrial electron transport chain and oxidative phosphorylation. This leads to cellular hypoxia and depletion of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). This leads to metabolic acidosis and a shift from aerobic to increased anaerobic metabolism. [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cyanide toxicity is a rare but often fatal poisoning that occurs through ingestion, inhalation, dermal absorption, or injection. Historically, cyanide toxicity has been used in mass suicides, individual murders, and chemical warfare. Ingestion and inhalation of toxic salts and gases are the most common sources of toxicity, respectively, though ingestion of plants, medications, and cyanogenic chemicals are alternative routes of exposure. Symptoms vary across organ systems, affecting the neurological, respiratory, cardiovascular, and gastrointestinal systems.

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ingestion and inhalation of toxic salts and gases are the most common sources of toxicity, respectively, though ingestion of plants, medications, and cyanogenic chemicals are alternative routes of exposure. [3] [4] Dermal absorption and injection are rare but possible sources of cyanide exposure. Ingestion is commonly found in suicide attempts, while inhalation exposures are often the result of structural fires. Cyanide salts, eg, potassium cyanide, are the most common type of cyanide ingestant, though exposure to cyanogenic glycosides in plants like cassava root can be fatal as well. [5] The combustion of synthetic plastics in structural fires releases hydrogen cyanide gas, making it the most common source of inhalation exposure. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Inhalation of cyanide gas can result in symptoms within seconds, suggesting rapid absorption. Ingestion can result in symptoms within minutes to hours. Limited data are available on dermal absorption; however, case reports suggest symptoms may occur within minutes to hours as well. [18] Intravenous administration of sodium nitroprusside at therapeutic doses administered over multiple days can result in cyanide toxicity within days. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Sodium Nitroprusside ," for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, peripheral oxygen delivery and utilization are impaired with the arrest of oxidative phosphorylation secondary to cyanide. The resulting arterialization of venous blood, in which the concentration of venous oxygen resembles that of arterial blood, is indicative of cyanide toxicity. A central venous oxygen saturation >90% is suggestive of cyanide toxicity but may also be present in both hydrogen sulfide and carbon monoxide toxicity. [25] Furthermore, venous blood may appear bright red, similar to arterial blood, due to the failure of peripheral oxygen utilization and the subsequent high oxygen content in venous blood.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxocobalamin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxocobalamin can serve as an antidote for cyanide poisoning. [2] Cyanide binds to the cytochrome c oxidase, a terminal complex in the electron transport chain. This process leads to inhibition of ATP production and cellular oxygen utilization. As a result, cellular respiration becomes blocked, resulting in rapid death. Hydroxocobalamin contains cobalt compounds that can bind to and detoxify cyanide by intercepting the cyanide before it inhibits cellular respiration. [8] The hydroxocobalamin rapidly forms cyanocobalamin by removing the cyanide from tissues. The kidneys then excrete the cyanocobalamin in the urine. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- History and Physical -- Clinical History
retrieved_docs.content: Proper treatment of cyanide exposure requires a thorough history and a complete physical exam. First, the treating team should determine the route of exposure, as this can predict the clinical course. The most common routes of exposure are ingestion and inhalation, while dermal exposure is less common. Additionally, the team should obtain information about how much cyanide salt the patient ingested or how long they were exposed to cyanide gas.

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Cyanide Antidotes
retrieved_docs.content: In conjunction with the assessment of the airway, breathing, and circulation, the healthcare team should rapidly assess their suspicion of cyanide exposure and the need for an antidote. The first-line antidote in the United States is hydroxycobalamin, usually in the form of the Cyanokit. Standard dosing is 5 g administered intravenously over 15 minutes with an additional possible 5 g dose. This 10 g total dose is approximately 5,000 times the recommended daily dose of vitamin B12. Hydroxycobalamin is preferred over sodium thiosulfate and sodium nitrite due to its minimal side effect profile, which is typically limited to discoloration of the skin or urine, although it may also interfere with certain laboratory tests. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Evaluation and Treatment of Cyanide Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are several commercial antidotes available for cyanide poisoning. The one that is utilized the most is Hydroxycobalamin which is given intravenously to the poisoning victim. Hydroxocobalamin binds to cyanide, forming cyanocobalamine (vitamin B-12), which is no longer toxic and is excreted by the kidneys. Endogenous detoxification of cyanide occurs via the rhodanese reaction. This reaction allows the thiosulfate ions to react with cyanide ions to form sulfite and thiocyanate. The thiocyanate is then excreted via the kidneys. Rhodanese is located in the mitochondria in the liver, kidneys, and skeletal muscle. Organs which have low concentrations of rhodanese are more prone to damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: The main pathway by which cyanide is eliminated from the body is via conversion to thiocyanate. The enzyme thiosulfate cyanide sulfurtransferase, also known as rhodanese, catalyzes the primary elimination pathway. This enzyme catalyzes thiosulfate sulfane sulfur group donation to cyanide to form thiocyanate. In severe exposures, thiosulfate stores are depleted, and cyanide elimination is minimal. Additional pathways of elimination exist, including via administration of hydroxocobalamin, which chelates cyanide to form cyanocobalamin. Both of these products are excreted in the urine. [30] [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cyanide toxicity is a rare, often fatal poisoning that patients are exposed to through various modes of entry and circumstances. It has been used historically in mass suicides such as the ingestions in the Jonestown mass suicide in 1978, as an agent for individual murders, and as a chemical weapon in gaseous form on battlefields and in the form of Zyklon B during the Holocaust. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanide Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acute cyanide exposure from inhalation often occurs concomitantly with carbon monoxide exposure in the setting of structural fires. Patients with carbon monoxide and cyanide toxicity alike can present with evidence of end-organ damage, hypoxia, hemodynamic instability, and altered mental status. Historically, a bitter almond scent and cherry-red skin were used as indicators of cyanide toxicity, but these are not reliable physical exam findings. [8] Hydrogen sulfide exposure exerts toxicity by a similar cellular mechanism to cyanide and should remain on the differential for undifferentiated patients with unexplained severe hemodynamic instability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 279
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxokobalamin, a hydroxylated form of vitamin B12, may be used as a therapeutic agent for the treatment of poisoning (التسمم). By binding toxic substances such as cyanide, hydroxokobalamin facilitates their conversion into non‑toxic metabolites that can be safely eliminated from the body, making it a valuable option in the management of acute toxic exposures. Its potential use in poisoning underscores the importance of recognizing specific antidotes in emergency and critical care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon monoxide (oxid uhelnatý) is classified as a gasotransmitter, a small gaseous signaling molecule involved in physiological processes. In addition to its signaling role, carbon monoxide is closely associated with carbon monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), a toxic condition that occurs when inhaled concentrations are high enough to impair oxygen delivery and cause cellular hypoxia. Understanding carbon monoxide’s dual nature as both a biologically active gasotransmitter and a potential toxin is important for clinical assessment, prevention of exposure, and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: mechanisms; tolvaptan is an example of a drug in this class. Carbon monoxide (CO) is positioned as a parent entity within the families of gases and oxygen‑containing compounds and is clinically significant as the cause of carbon‑monoxide poisoning. CO is conceptually linked to acid‑base markers and blood‑gas measurements, as well as to the related gases oxygen (kyslík) and carbon dioxide (oxid uhličitý). Both oxygen and carbon dioxide are subcategories of the broader “gases” group, and carbon dioxide serves as a parent of inorganic carbon compounds and of “oxygen‑containing compounds.” Oxygen therapy is the primary treatment for carbon‑monoxide poisoning, providing essential oxygen to the patient. A prior misclassification that placed antimetabolites as a subtype of carbon monoxide has been corrected; antimetabolites remain a distinct pharmacologic class separate from the inorganic gas and its toxicological profile. The combined relationships illustrate how drug classes

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1527
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “Unknown Cellular or Molecular Interaction [MoA]” is identified as the mechanism of action for both merkaptamin and jodid draselný (potassium iodide). Although the precise biochemical pathways of this interaction remain undefined, the fact that it underlies the pharmacologic effects of these two distinct agents suggests a shared, yet not fully characterized, mode of action. Recognizing this common, unknown mechanism may guide future research aimed at elucidating the molecular basis of merkaptamin and potassium iodide activity and could have implications for optimizing their clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon monoxide, referred to in Czech as oxid uhelnatý, is classified both as a gasotransmitter and as an antimetabolite. As a gasotransmitter, it functions as a small, endogenously produced gaseous signaling molecule that can modulate vascular tone, neurotransmission, and inflammatory responses. Its antimetabolite properties allow it to interfere with normal metabolic pathways, for example by binding to heme‑containing enzymes and altering cellular respiration. Exposure to carbon monoxide is directly associated with carbon‑monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), a clinical syndrome characterized by hypoxic injury, neurological deficits, and potentially fatal outcomes. Recognizing carbon monoxide’s dual role as a physiological messenger and a toxic agent is essential for accurate diagnosis, prevention, and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1673
retrieved_docs.content: Kyslík is classified as a member of the broader category plyny, indicating that it is one of the fundamental gases. In the clinical context of التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون (carbon‑monoxide poisoning), administration of kyslík serves as a primary therapeutic intervention; oxygen therapy displaces carbon monoxide from hemoglobin, restores adequate tissue oxygenation, and mitigates the toxic effects of the poisoning. Recognizing kyslík’s place within plyny and its essential role in treating carbon‑monoxide poisoning supports effective patient management and underscores the interconnected nature of basic chemical classification and clinical treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: compound; its clinical significance is highlighted by carbon‑monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), which impairs oxygen delivery by binding hemoglobin with higher affinity than oxygen. Products containing carbon or carbon‑based compounds are organized under inorganic carbon compounds (anorganické sloučeniny uhlíku), which are descendants of carbon dioxide. The radioactive isotope carbon‑14 is a specific form of inorganic carbon, linking it to the same hierarchical lineage. Natriuretic substances (natriuretické látky) include natriuretic peptide type B as a specific subclass involved in sodium homeostasis and cardiovascular regulation. Vasopressin‑V2 receptor antagonists (antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu) are classified within natriuretic substances because they promote aquaresis without significant sodium loss. Their molecular mechanism involves selective blockade of V2 receptors in the renal collecting ducts, inhibition of antidiuretic hormone action, reduction of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 423
retrieved_docs.content: Acid‑base markers and blood gases are clinical parameters that reflect the status of key gaseous molecules in the circulatory system—carbon monoxide (CO), oxygen (O₂), and carbon dioxide (CO₂). CO, oxygen, and CO₂ belong to the broader families of oxides, gases, and oxygen‑containing compounds; CO and CO₂ are also classified as carbon‑containing products and inorganic carbon compounds, linking them to the radioactive isotope carbon‑14. CO functions as a gasotransmitter, an inverse subclass of signaling molecules, and is the causative agent in carbon monoxide poisoning. O₂ is the therapeutic agent used to treat carbon monoxide poisoning, highlighting the clinical interplay among these gases. CO₂ is specifically identified as an oxygen compound, reinforcing its classification within the larger group of oxygen compounds. This integrated view underscores the physiological, toxicological, and diagnostic relevance of carbon‑based gases, their inorganic forms, and related biomarkers in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: an antimetabolite and a gasotransmitter; it is the cause of carbon‑monoxide poisoning, which is managed with oxygen therapy. CO, oxygen and carbon dioxide (CO₂) are all gases and oxygen‑containing compounds; CO₂ originates from inorganic carbon, and acid‑base markers and blood gases are used to assess O₂ and CO₂ status. CO also serves as a parent concept for acid‑base markers, blood gases, inorganic carbon and related inorganic carbon compounds, underscoring its relevance to metabolic and respiratory physiology. A chemical‑structure hierarchy groups nitrogen‑based compounds—amrinon, colestipol, acetylcholin, chlorzoxazon, haloperidol and moxisylyte—highlighting shared pharmacokinetic considerations. The vitamin hierarchy places cholin (acetylcholine) as a parent of potassium aminobenzoate, vitamin K₁, K₂, K₃, vitamins A and D (including their combinations), and vitamin B₁ (thiamine) alone or combined with B₆ and B₁₂. Vitamins are linked by “related_to” relationships to nutritional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 74
retrieved_docs.content: dosing schedules, stability, and tolerability. Inorganic and gaseous qualifiers include oxygen (kyslík) and carbon dioxide (oxid uhličitý), both classified as therapeutic gases; oxygen is employed in carbon‑monoxide poisoning, while carbon dioxide belongs to the oxygen‑compound family and underlies inorganic carbon, generic carbon‑containing products, and the radionuclide carbon‑14. Toxicological agents such as carbon monoxide, antimetabolites, and certain inorganic compounds (copper sulfate) are also linked to application and dosage, requiring safety monitoring and specific antidotal strategies. Radiopharmaceuticals are recognized as a distinct child of the molecular‑mechanism category “pharmacological effects – molecular mechanisms,” reflecting their unique dosing considerations. Membrane‑transport modulators (e.g., FMRFamid) and other molecular‑mechanism groups connect antimetabolites to downstream hypolipidemic agents. Dermatological substances form a sub‑category of medications

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 92
retrieved_docs.content: the anticholinergic pharmacologic class. Collectively, these entities and relationships demonstrate that alcohol, cocaine, heroin, hallucinogens, opioids, and other psychoactive drugs are interlinked through shared diagnostic categories, dependence mechanisms, poisoning risks, and pharmacologic interventions, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and prevention of toxic complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: illustrate how ascorbic acid and its derivatives intersect with ophthalmic preparations, vitamin C supplementation, and iron‑deficiency treatment, while also showing that certain ophthalmic agents (merkaptamin and potassium iodide) possess mechanisms of action that remain to be clarified. This integrated classification aids clinicians in understanding the multiple therapeutic contexts in which these substances are employed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 356
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid uhelnatý, commonly known as carbon monoxide, is identified both as a gasotransmitery and as an antimetabolity. As a gasotransmitery, carbon monoxide functions as a small gaseous signaling molecule that influences vascular tone, neurotransmission, and anti‑inflammatory pathways in normal physiology. In its role as an antimetabolity, carbon monoxide interferes with cellular metabolic processes by binding to heme‑containing enzymes and altering cellular respiration, which can contribute to both therapeutic effects and toxic outcomes. Recognizing carbon monoxide’s dual classification as a gasotransmitery and an antimetabolity highlights its importance in understanding its physiological signaling functions, potential pharmacologic applications, and the management of exposure‑related toxicity in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 69
retrieved_docs.content: Antimetabolites are a pharmacologic class that interferes with normal nucleotide metabolism and are linked to the broader “noxy” molecular‑mechanism group. Representative agents include the purine‑based compounds 2‑Aminopurine, afidikolin and azathioprine sodium, which act as purine antagonists, as well as nucleoside analogues such as deoxyuridine (a precursor of the antiviral idoxuridine), deoxyglucose, dideoxyinosine, cycloserine and ethionine; each mimics natural metabolites and disrupts essential biosynthetic pathways. Membrane‑transport modulators constitute another mechanistic category that alters ion‑channel and transporter activity. Potassium‑channel blockers are a child class of this group, and the peptide‑derived agent FMRFamid is also classified as a membrane‑transport modulator; both fall under the parent concept “pharmacological effects – molecular mechanisms.” Carbon monoxide (CO) is classified as a gasotransmitter, belonging to the families of gases and oxygen‑containing compounds. CO’s high affinity for hemoglobin produces hypoxic injury in carbon‑monoxide poisoning, a clinical condition that illustrates the toxic interaction between CO and oxygen. In respiratory physiology, CO, oxygen (O₂) and carbon dioxide (CO₂) are integral to blood‑gas analysis and acid‑base assessment, and both CO and CO₂ are linked chemically to inorganic carbon compounds within the inorganic carbon system. Together, the network connects antimetabolite drugs that target purine metabolism, membrane‑transport modulators that influence ion‑channel function, and gaseous molecules such as CO that serve both signaling and toxic roles. Understanding these interrelationships supports clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies, monitoring respiratory and metabolic parameters, and recognizing the hazards of gas‑related toxicities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2061
retrieved_docs.content: Anorganické sloučeniny uhlíku (inorganic carbon compounds) include oxid uhelnatý (carbon monoxide) as a representative member, indicating that carbon monoxide is classified among simple inorganic compounds containing carbon. In addition, oxid uhelnatý is also regarded as an antimetabolity (antimetabolite), reflecting its ability to act as a metabolic inhibitor that can interfere with normal cellular biochemical pathways. This dual categorization underscores carbon monoxide’s chemical identity as an inorganic carbon‑containing molecule and its biological relevance as a compound that can disrupt metabolism, which is important for understanding both its toxicological effects and potential therapeutic applications.
